glossary.def: Any path by which information unavailable at real prediction time — the label itself, a future value, or statistics computed across the split — reaches the model and inflates its measured performance. It is the canonical reason a model scores well in the notebook and collapses in production, and it is detected by process auditing, not by correlation analysis.

A specific leak where a feature is a consequence of the label rather than a cause knowable beforehand (e.g. a treatment column used to predict the diagnosis that prompted it). The feature is enormously predictive on held-out data yet useless in deployment, because at prediction time the label has not yet been realized.

Information from the future bleeding into the past — most often by fitting a scaler, imputer, or encoder on the full dataset before splitting, so training rows are transformed using statistics that include the test rows. The cure is to split first and fit all preprocessing on the training partition only.

Leakage caused by a random split scattering rows that belong to the same entity (patient, user, document) across train and test, so the model is tested on entities it has effectively already seen. The fix is a grouped split that keeps every entity wholly on one side of the boundary.

A disjoint three-way partition of a dataset: training fits parameters, validation selects hyperparameters and models, and test — touched exactly once — estimates generalization. Tuning against the test set destroys its value; its entire worth is that nothing downstream of it ever saw the test data.

A resampling protocol that rotates the validation role across k disjoint folds, training k times and averaging, so every row serves for both training and validation (never simultaneously). It gives a lower-variance performance estimate than a single split at k× the compute, and assumes the rows are exchangeable.

Any mismatch between the training, test, and deployment distributions that breaks the i.i.d. assumption underlying evaluation. Covariate shift moves p(x), label shift moves p(y), and concept drift moves p(y|x); a random split hides all three because it makes train and test identical by construction.

The first-class pipeline step of verifying that no training example, or a trivial variant of one, also appears in the validation or test set — typically via row hashing. Eval contamination silently turns measured generalization into memorization and is the same discipline that fine-tuning a model demands against its benchmarks.

refs.cite: Kaufman, S., Rosset, S., Perlich, C. & Stitelman, O. (2012). Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, and Avoidance. ACM TKDD 6(4).
Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R. & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). Springer.
Northcutt, C. G., Athalye, A. & Mueller, J. (2021). Pervasive Label Errors in Test Sets Destabilize Machine Learning Benchmarks. NeurIPS Datasets & Benchmarks.
Obermeyer, Z., Powers, B., Vogeli, C. & Mullainathan, S. (2019). Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm Used to Manage the Health of Populations. Science 366(6464).
Cawley, G. C. & Talbot, N. L. C. (2010). On Over-fitting in Model Selection and Subsequent Selection Bias in Performance Evaluation. JMLR 11.
Quiñonero-Candela, J., Sugiyama, M., Schwaighofer, A. & Lawrence, N. D. (2009). Dataset Shift in Machine Learning. MIT Press.
